Title: China Slowdown Hammers Commodity Currencies
Summary: AUD and NZD slide as China's manufacturing PMI contracts for a third straight month.
Content: China's official manufacturing PMI dropped to 48.7 in February, the third consecutive sub-50 reading, intensifying fears of a prolonged economic slowdown in the world's second-largest economy. Commodity-linked currencies bore the brunt of the sell-off: AUD/USD fell 0.9% to 0.6312 while NZD/USD shed 1.1%. Iron ore futures slid 3.4% in Dalian, amplifying the pain for the Australian dollar. Analysts at major banks are now revising China's full-year GDP forecasts below the official 5% target, citing weak domestic consumption, a moribund property sector, and persistent deflationary pressures. Capital flight from Chinese equity markets has added further headwinds, with the Hang Seng Index logging its seventh decline in eight sessions.
Author: FX News Team

Title: Taiwan Strait Tensions Spark Safe-Haven Rally
Summary: JPY, CHF and gold surge as military posturing in the Taiwan Strait intensifies.
Content: Heightened military activity near the Taiwan Strait triggered a broad flight to safety in global currency markets. The Japanese yen surged 1.4% against the dollar, pushing USD/JPY below 145, while the Swiss franc appreciated 0.8%. Gold climbed to $2,420 per troy ounce, its highest level since November. Analysts warn that any escalation could severely disrupt global semiconductor supply chains, with Taiwan accounting for over 60% of advanced chip manufacturing capacity. The Taiwan dollar weakened sharply, and regional equity indices in South Korea, Japan and Australia all closed more than 1.5% lower. Strategists at several investment banks are recommending reducing EM exposure while increasing allocations to traditional safe-haven assets.
Author: FX News Team

Title: PBOC Cuts Reserve Requirements Amid Growth Fears
Summary: China's central bank eases policy as GDP targets look increasingly out of reach.
Content: The People's Bank of China announced a 50 basis point cut to the reserve requirement ratio (RRR) for most banks, releasing an estimated CNY 1 trillion of liquidity into a struggling economy. The move came days after data showed retail sales growth slowed to its weakest pace in over a year and industrial output contracted for the first time since the pandemic. While the easing was broadly expected, market participants expressed concern that monetary stimulus alone cannot address the structural headwinds facing China: an overleveraged property sector, falling consumer confidence, and slowing export demand from a fragile global economy. The offshore yuan (CNH) initially rallied on the news before giving back gains, and Chinese government bond yields fell to a two-decade low as investors priced in a prolonged low-growth environment.
Author: FX News Team

Title: US Expands Tariffs on Chinese Goods, Renewing Trade War Fears
Summary: Washington's new 25% tariffs on Chinese tech exports send USD higher and EM currencies lower.
Content: The United States announced sweeping new 25% tariffs on a broad range of Chinese-manufactured technology products, including electric vehicles, solar panels and advanced semiconductors. The move, framed as a national security measure, drew an immediate pledge of retaliation from Beijing. The US dollar index (DXY) jumped 0.7% as investors priced in a more restrictive global trade environment, while the offshore yuan weakened past 7.35 against the dollar — a level not seen since the 2022 peak. Emerging market currencies with close trade ties to China, including the Thai baht, South Korean won and Malaysian ringgit, fell sharply. Equity markets in Europe and Asia sold off, with tech-heavy indices leading losses on fears of supply-chain disruption. Strategists warn that a full-blown trade war could shave 0.5–0.8 percentage points from global GDP growth in 2026.
Author: FX News Team

Title: China Property Crisis Deepens as Evergrande Sequel Unfolds
Summary: Second major Chinese developer halts offshore debt payments, triggering global risk-off.
Content: Country Garden, once China's largest property developer by sales, has formally defaulted on $3.5 billion in offshore bonds after failing to secure a creditor agreement in court-supervised restructuring talks. The news revived memories of the Evergrande collapse and sent shockwaves through global credit markets. High-yield spreads widened sharply in Asia and Europe, the Australian dollar fell 1.2% on fears of reduced Chinese demand for raw materials, and Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index plunged more than 3%. Chinese authorities face growing pressure to launch a direct government bailout of the sector, which historically accounted for about a quarter of GDP. Analysts caution that without a credible resolution mechanism, the property sector overhang could weigh on Chinese growth for years, with knock-on effects for global commodity markets and commodity-linked currencies.
Author: FX News Team

Title: China GDP May Be 1–2% Lower Than Official Data, Morningstar Estimates
Summary: Independent modelling raises doubts about reported growth figures as leading indicators diverge.
Content: Morningstar's economics research team has published an analysis suggesting China's actual GDP growth in 2025 may have been 1 to 2 percentage points below the official 5.2% figure, based on satellite data tracking industrial activity, electricity consumption, freight volumes and night-light intensity. The divergence between soft survey data and hard activity indicators has widened to levels not seen outside of major economic shocks. Morningstar analysts flag that China's methodology for calculating GDP differs meaningfully from international standards, particularly in how it treats the services sector and government-funded infrastructure spending. The firm has revised its 2026 China GDP forecast to 3.6%, well below the government's 5% target, and warns that currency and equity markets may not fully reflect this downside risk.
Author: Morningstar Research

Title: Geopolitical Risk Premium: Positioning in an Unstable World
Summary: Morningstar lays out a framework for portfolio resilience amid rising global tensions.
Content: In its latest quarterly strategy note, Morningstar Investment Management argues that investors have chronically underpriced geopolitical risk across asset classes. With conflicts active or simmering in Ukraine, the Middle East and the Taiwan Strait simultaneously, the firm recommends a 'geopolitical resilience' framework: overweighting hard assets (gold, energy infrastructure), reducing duration in government bonds from countries with high foreign ownership, and diversifying currency exposure away from CNY and RUB. The note highlights that the JPY and CHF have historically delivered the best risk-adjusted returns during geopolitical stress events, and that investors should treat volatility spikes as entry opportunities. Morningstar also flags that rare earth supply chain risks — with China controlling roughly 85% of global processing capacity — represent a structural risk to tech and defence equities that the market has yet to fully price.
Author: Morningstar Research

Title: China's Rare Earth Export Controls: A New Economic Weapon
Summary: Beijing's restrictions on critical mineral exports could reshape global supply chains and lift commodity prices.
Content: China's Ministry of Commerce has expanded export licensing requirements to cover 17 rare earth elements and 12 critical minerals used in electric vehicle batteries, wind turbines and advanced military systems. Morningstar analysts describe the move as 'weaponising the supply chain', echoing the pattern set by earlier restrictions on gallium and germanium. The immediate market impact was a 12% rally in rare earth miner equities listed in Australia and Canada. Longer-term, Morningstar warns that Western governments lack the capacity to substitute Chinese supply within a 5-year horizon, making inflationary pressures in clean-energy and defence manufacturing likely. The restrictions add a new dimension to an already fractious US-China trade relationship and could accelerate the decoupling of global technology supply chains — a trend with profound implications for EM currencies tied to Chinese trade flows.
Author: Morningstar Research

Title: BRICS Expansion and the Long Road to Dollar Alternatives
Summary: New BRICS members boost trade settlement ambitions, but dollar hegemony faces no immediate threat.
Content: Following the formal admission of six new member states to the BRICS bloc — including Saudi Arabia, Iran, Ethiopia and Argentina — the group has accelerated discussions on a common trade settlement currency. Morningstar's currency strategy team argues that while the political will exists, the technical and institutional barriers to displacing the US dollar remain formidable: there is no unified BRICS capital market, no common central bank and significant divergence in monetary policy frameworks across members. In the near term, the expansion is most likely to increase bilateral trade settled in local currencies — particularly CNY, INR and BRL — at the margins. Morningstar estimates that a 5-percentage-point shift away from dollar settlement would reduce global USD demand by roughly $120 billion annually, a non-trivial but manageable headwind. Investors are advised to monitor CNY internationalisation metrics closely for signs of a more structural shift.
Author: Morningstar Research

Title: Renminbi Hits Multi-Year Low as Capital Outflows Accelerate
Summary: USD/CNH breaks 7.40 as foreign investors pull out of Chinese equities and bonds at record pace.
Content: The offshore renminbi weakened past 7.40 per dollar for the first time in over seven years, as foreign institutional investors reduced their China exposure at the fastest pace since 2015. Bloomberg data shows that offshore bond portfolios have seen net outflows of $42 billion over the past three months, driven by a combination of narrowing yield differentials, geopolitical risk premia and concerns about China's economic trajectory. The PBOC has been intervening via state banks to smooth the depreciation, but analysts say the central bank's reserves buffer, while substantial at $3.2 trillion, is being tested. A weaker yuan adds to imported inflation pressures and complicates the PBOC's easing cycle. USD/CNH volatility has risen to its highest level since the 2022 COVID lockdowns, and currency options desks report elevated demand for protection against further CNH weakness.
Author: Bloomberg Markets

Title: Fed Holds Rates as Trade War Clouds Economic Outlook
Summary: FOMC keeps policy on hold, citing heightened uncertainty from US-China tariff escalation.
Content: The Federal Open Market Committee voted unanimously to hold the federal funds rate target at 4.25–4.50%, citing 'elevated uncertainty in the trade and geopolitical environment' as a key reason for caution. Chair Powell noted at the post-meeting press conference that the committee sees risks to both sides of its dual mandate: renewed tariff-driven inflation could slow the pace of rate cuts, while slowing global growth — particularly from China — could weaken domestic demand more than anticipated. Bloomberg Economics estimates that the latest round of US-China tariffs could add 0.4 percentage points to core PCE inflation over the next 12 months while simultaneously subtracting 0.3 points from GDP growth, a stagflationary combination that limits the Fed's room to manoeuvre. The dollar index edged higher after the decision as markets pushed back their expectations for a first 25 basis point cut to September.
Author: Bloomberg Markets

Title: South China Sea Escalation: Markets Price In Risk Premium
Summary: Naval standoff near the Spratly Islands pushes risk assets lower and safe-havens higher.
Content: A naval standoff between Chinese and Philippine coast guard vessels near the Spratly Islands escalated over the weekend, with the United States deploying two carrier strike groups to the Western Pacific in a show of support for its treaty ally. Bloomberg terminal data shows that credit default swap spreads on Philippine sovereign debt have widened 35 basis points in two sessions, while the Philippine peso has shed 2.1% against the dollar. Regional shipping rates have risen 8% as vessels seek alternative routes, adding to inflationary pressures for Asian manufacturers. Gold and US Treasuries are the clear beneficiaries, with 10-year Treasury yields falling 12 basis points as safe-haven demand swamps the inflation narrative. Geopolitical strategists at Bloomberg Intelligence warn that the situation risks drawing in broader regional actors and triggering a more sustained risk-off episode across Asian financial markets.
Author: Bloomberg Markets

Title: Dollar Dominance Intact Despite BRICS Push, Bloomberg Survey Shows
Summary: Central bank reserve managers overwhelmingly favour USD despite de-dollarisation rhetoric.
Content: A Bloomberg survey of 57 central bank reserve managers representing over $8 trillion in foreign exchange reserves finds that 82% plan to maintain or increase their USD allocations over the next 12 months, suggesting that de-dollarisation remains more rhetorical than real. While the share of USD in global reserves has declined from 72% in 2000 to around 58% today, there is no credible near-term challenger: the euro remains constrained by fragmented bond markets, the renminbi by capital controls, and gold by its non-yielding nature. Respondents cited deep US Treasury market liquidity as the single most important factor in their reserve allocation decisions. However, the survey also shows growing interest in CNY-denominated instruments among respondents in the Middle East and Southeast Asia, suggesting that incremental diversification — rather than a wholesale dollar exodus — is the more likely scenario over a 5-to-10-year horizon.
Author: Bloomberg Markets
